attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old man is admitted to the rehabilitation ward following a hyperextension injury to his cervical spine three weeks ago when he fell backwards down stairs. He sustained a central cord syndrome at C4-5 level with initial magnetic resonance imaging showing cervical spondylosis and cord signal changes. He has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. He has American Spinal Injury Association (ASIA) grade D symptoms with motor scores of 3/5 in upper limbs and 4/5 in lower limbs. He has sacral sparing and can walk with minimal assistance. He demonstrates good functional improvement with rehabilitation. Repeat imaging shows stable cervical spondylosis with mild ongoing cord compression at C4-5.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Urgent repeat MRI imaging of the cervical spine
Surgical decompression six months after initial injury
Transfer to spinal unit for surgical decompression
Cervical collar immobilisation for three months with ongoing rehabilitation
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with central cord syndrome, which is the most common spinal cord injury syndrome. The characteristic pattern shows greater weakness in the upper limbs compared to lower limbs. The patient is showing significant improvement with ASIA grade D status, indicating functional motor strength below the level of injury. There is ongoing debate regarding conservative vs surgical management for central cord syndrome, with both approaches achieving possible neurological recovery. Surgical management is considered standard in those with ongoing mechanical instability and appears to yield improvements in outcomes in those with more severe neurological impairment.

Ongoing rehabilitation is the most appropriate management given the patient's continued neurological improvement and functional gains. When patients demonstrate good recovery without spinal instability or neurological deterioration, conservative management with close monitoring is the preferred approach. Regular assessment ensures early detection of any deterioration that would necessitate surgical intervention.

Urgent repeat MRI imaging is not indicated as the patient has already had recent imaging showing stable findings, and there are no new neurological symptoms or signs of deterioration that would warrant urgent reimaging.

Surgical decompression six months after initial injury would only be considered if the patient's recovery plateaued or if they developed late neurological deterioration. The timing of surgery in central cord syndrome remains controversial, but there is no evidence supporting routine delayed surgery in patients who are improving with conservative management.

Transfer to spinal unit for surgical decompression would be indicated for patients with significant neurological deterioration, spinal instability, or severe cord compression causing progressive symptoms. This patient's stable condition and ongoing improvement do not warrant urgent surgical intervention.

Cervical collar immobilisation for three months is unnecessary as there is no evidence of spinal instability. Prolonged immobilisation may actually impede rehabilitation efforts and functional recovery. In the initial acute management of central cord syndrome, cervical spine immobilisation remains a key management strategy to reduce further spinal injury.

The key to management is regular monitoring of neurological status while continuing rehabilitation. Surgical intervention should be reserved for cases with neurological deterioration, significant instability, or failure to improve with conservative management.
learningPoints: Conservative management is appropriate for central cord syndrome when neurological improvement is occurring and there are no signs of instability.
Central cord syndrome typically presents with greater upper limb weakness than lower limb weakness following hyperextension injury.
Regular neurological monitoring is essential to detect any deterioration that would prompt surgical intervention.
references: Divi SN, Schroeder GD, Mangan JJ, et al. Management of Acute Traumatic Central Cord Syndrome: A Narrative Review. Global Spine Journal. 2019;9(1_suppl):89S-97S. doi:10.1177/2192568219830943. Available online at ; https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/2192568219830943

Kepler CK, Kong C, Schroeder GD, et al. Early outcome and predictors of early outcome in patients treated surgically for central cord syndrome. Journal of Neurosurgery: Spine SPI. 2015;23(4):490-494. doi:10.3171/2015.1.SPINE141013. Available online at ; https://thejns.org/spine/view/journals/j-neurosurg-spine/23/4/article-p490.xml

hou Q, He W, Lv J, Liu H, Yang H, Zhang J, Liu T. Benefits of early surgical treatment for patients with multilevel cervical canal stenosis of acute traumatic central cord syndrome. Orthopaedic Surgery. 2023 Dec;15(12):3092-100. Available online at ; https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdfdirect/10.1111/os.13904
